era_context: Mid-14th century: the Ilkhanate (Mongol state in Persia) is in decline; the Yuan Dynasty in China is weakening; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Black Death is spreading from Central Asia; the Avignon Papacy continues; the Mamluk Sultanate controls Egypt and the Levant; England and France are moving toward conflict.

events.title: Ilkhanate collapses in Persia
events.description: The Ilkhanate, the Mongol state that ruled Persia and surrounding regions, collapses in 1335 with the death of the last Ilkhan, Abu Sa'id. The collapse of the Ilkhanate marks the end of Mongol rule in Persia and leads to political fragmentation. Multiple successor states and dynasties emerge to fill the power vacuum, including the Timurid Empire (which will be founded by Timur in the 1360s). The collapse of the Ilkhanate has significant consequences for trade routes, regional politics, and the balance of power in the Middle East and Central Asia.

sources.name: Persian chronicles (Rashid al-Din, Jami' al-tawarikh; Wassaf)

events.certainty_note: The collapse of the Ilkhanate in 1335 with the death of Abu Sa'id is well-documented in Persian chronicles and confirmed by modern scholarship. The date is firmly established.

events.title: Black Death spreads through Central Asia and the Middle East
events.region: Central Asia, Middle East, Silk Road trade routes
events.description: The Black Death continues its spread westward from Central Asia in 1335. The plague is carried along the Silk Road trade routes by merchants, armies, and travellers. Central Asian cities and trade hubs experience massive mortality. The plague's spread is facilitated by the extensive Mongol Empire's trade networks. By 1335, the plague is approaching the Middle East and North Africa. The disease will eventually reach Europe in 1347, where it will cause devastating mortality. The plague's spread is one of the most significant demographic events of the medieval period.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus from modern epidemiological studies

events.certainty_note: The Black Death's presence in Central Asia and the Middle East in 1335 is supported by modern epidemiological models, but contemporary documentation is sparse. The exact timeline of the plague's spread is debated among historians.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty faces widespread rebellion and economic instability
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty (Mongol rule) faces widespread rebellion and economic instability in 1335. Multiple rebel groups challenge Yuan authority across China. The government's control is weakening, particularly in southern China. Economic problems, including inflation and currency debasement, are destabilizing the state. These uprisings are precursors to the eventual fall of the Yuan Dynasty. The rebellions are driven by economic hardship, resentment of Mongol rule, and the spread of secret societies and religious movements. Zhu Yuanzhang is among the rebel leaders gaining prominence during this period.
events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty instability and rebellions in the 1330s are well-attested, but specific events in 1335 are less precisely dated. The Ming Shilu was compiled retrospectively and may conflate events.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates power in Anatolia
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Orhan I (r. 1323–1362), continues to consolidate power in Anatolia in 1335. Ottoman forces are expanding territory at the expense of neighbouring Beylik states and weakened Byzantine territories. The Ottoman state is becoming increasingly organized and militarized. Orhan is establishing a more formal administrative structure and military system. The Ottomans are transitioning from a small principality to a major regional power. This period marks the beginning of Ottoman expansion that will eventually lead to the conquest of Constantinople.
events.certainty_note: Orhan I's consolidation of Ottoman power in the 1330s is attested in later Ottoman chronicles and Byzantine sources, but specific events in 1335 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate maintains control of Egypt and the Levant
events.description: The Mamluk Sultanate, under Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad (r. 1310–1341, with interruptions), continues to maintain control of Egypt and the Levant in 1335. The Mamluk state is a major regional power, controlling trade routes and maintaining military dominance. The Sultanate's wealth is derived from control of the spice trade and agricultural production in the Nile Delta. Internal factional conflicts among the Mamluk elite are ongoing, but the state remains stable and powerful. The Mamluk military system, based on slave soldiers, remains effective.
events.certainty_note: The Mamluk Sultanate's control of Egypt and the Levant in 1335 is well-established. Al-Nasir Muhammad's reign is documented in multiple sources.

events.title: Pope Benedict XII continues Avignon Papacy and church reforms
events.description: Pope Benedict XII (r. 1334–1342) continues the Avignon Papacy and pursues church reforms. Benedict XII is known for his efforts to address corruption within the clergy and to improve monastic discipline. He issues various bulls and decrees aimed at reforming the Church. The Avignon Papacy remains a source of controversy and criticism, with many viewing the papal residence in France as a sign of papal weakness and French political influence over the Church. Benedict XII's papacy is marked by attempts to strengthen papal authority and reform the Church hierarchy.

sources.name: Papal bulls and official documents (Vatican Archives)
Petrarch, Letters (contemporary criticism of the Avignon Papacy)

events.certainty_note: Benedict XII's papacy and his reform efforts are well-documented in papal records and contemporary correspondence.

disconfirming_evidence: The collapse of the Ilkhanate in 1335 is sometimes presented as a sudden event, but the decline of Ilkhanate power had been gradual over several decades. Abu Sa'id's death marked the formal end, but the state had been weakening for years. The Black Death's origin and early spread are debated; the exact timeline of its westward movement in 1335 is uncertain. Some scholars argue the plague originated in the Gobi Desert, others in Central Asia more broadly. Yuan Dynasty rebellions are sometimes conflated across multiple years in later sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1335 is moderate but heavily weighted toward Western European and Persian sources. English and French royal records are abundant. Persian chronicles documenting the collapse of the Ilkhanate are available but often compiled after the events. Sources from the Ottoman Empire and China are either later compilations or fragmentary. The Black Death's early spread is reconstructed from epidemiological models and later chronicles rather than contemporary records. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no contemporary written records for this year. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites and court-sponsored chroniclers.

graph_edges.note: The collapse of the Ilkhanate in 1335 created a power vacuum that Timur would eventually fill with the Timurid Empire in 1369
The Black Death's spread from Central Asia in 1335 led to its arrival in Europe in 1347
Yuan Dynasty rebellions in 1335 contributed to the eventual fall of the Yuan Dynasty in 1368
Ottoman consolidation in 1335 was part of the long process that led to the fall of Constantinople in 1453
